content.text: When a GenAI tool inventory reveals capability overlaps, the organisation must decide what to do about each one. A consolidation decision is not simply a cost optimisation — it involves trade-offs between spend reduction, migration risk, capability continuity, and team autonomy. Without a structured decision framework, rationalization reviews devolve into ad-hoc debates where the loudest team wins. The consolidation decision criteria define the factors that determine whether two overlapping tools should be consolidated, kept separately with a defined scope, or left as-is for intentional strategic reasons.
The Five Decision Dimensions
Every consolidation candidate is evaluated across five dimensions. No single dimension is sufficient on its own — the decision emerges from the combination.
Dimension
What to measure
Consolidate signal
Keep-separate signal
Capability overlap
Does the tool provide the same output as the primary standard for the same use cases?
Functional output is equivalent; teams use both tools for the same tasks
Tool covers a distinct modality or serves a use case the primary cannot (e.g. on-prem only, specific language support)
Combined spend
What is the total monthly cost of maintaining both tools simultaneously?
Combined spend > $2,000/month for the same capability — savings justify migration effort
Spend is negligible (< $200/month free-tier); consolidation cost exceeds savings over 12 months
Compliance status
Does the tool have a signed DPA, completed security questionnaire, and production approval?
Tool has compliance gaps — consolidation resolves the gap without needing remediation
Tool is compliant; compliance alone is not a reason to consolidate if capability is differentiated
Adoption depth
How many teams and active users depend on the tool in production?
Low adoption (1–2 teams, < 20 WAU) — migration is low-friction
High adoption (10+ teams, active integrations) — migration risk is significant; plan carefully
Migration cost
What is the effort and risk to migrate from the tool to the standard?
API-compatible or same-SDK migration; < 1 sprint per team
Requires significant refactoring, model re-evaluation, or retraining; risk of capability regression
Weighted Scoring Model

Applying the five dimensions consistently requires a scoring model. The model converts qualitative assessments into a numeric score so the rationalization review can prioritise which overlaps to address first and compare decisions across different capability areas.

# consolidation/scoring.py
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

OverlapRating = Literal["high", "medium", "low", "none"]

WEIGHT = {
    "capability_overlap":  0.30,   # most important: is there real duplication?
    "combined_spend":      0.25,   # financial materiality
    "compliance_gaps":     0.20,   # risk that must be resolved
    "migration_cost":      0.15,   # friction of change
    "adoption_depth":      0.10,   # inverse: high adoption = harder to consolidate
}

# Scores 0–10 for each dimension (higher = stronger consolidation signal)
CAPABILITY_OVERLAP_SCORE = {"high": 10, "medium": 6, "low": 2, "none": 0}
SPEND_SCORE  = {">10k": 10, "5-10k": 8, "2-5k": 6, "500-2k": 3, "<500": 1}
COMPLIANCE_SCORE = {
    "no_dpa_and_no_dd": 10,   # immediate action required
    "no_dpa_only":       8,
    "no_dd_only":        5,
    "compliant":         0,
}
MIGRATION_SCORE  = {"trivial": 10, "low": 7, "medium": 4, "high": 1, "very_high": 0}
# Adoption: inverse — high adoption = lower score (harder to consolidate)
ADOPTION_SCORE = {"very_low": 10, "low": 7, "medium": 4, "high": 1, "very_high": 0}

@dataclass
class ConsolidationCandidate:
    capability:         str
    tool_ids:           list[str]
    capability_overlap: OverlapRating          # "high" | "medium" | "low" | "none"
    combined_spend_band: str                   # ">10k" | "5-10k" | "2-5k" | "500-2k" | "<500"
    compliance_status:  str                    # see COMPLIANCE_SCORE keys
    migration_effort:   str                    # "trivial" | "low" | "medium" | "high" | "very_high"
    adoption_level:     str                    # "very_low" | "low" | "medium" | "high" | "very_high"
    notes:              str = ""

def score(candidate: ConsolidationCandidate) -> dict:
    raw = {
        "capability_overlap": CAPABILITY_OVERLAP_SCORE[candidate.capability_overlap],
        "combined_spend":     SPEND_SCORE[candidate.combined_spend_band],
        "compliance_gaps":    COMPLIANCE_SCORE[candidate.compliance_status],
        "migration_cost":     MIGRATION_SCORE[candidate.migration_effort],
        "adoption_depth":     ADOPTION_SCORE[candidate.adoption_level],
    }
    weighted = sum(raw[dim] * WEIGHT[dim] for dim in raw)
    return {
        "candidate":    candidate.capability,
        "tool_ids":     candidate.tool_ids,
        "dimension_scores": raw,
        "weighted_score":   round(weighted, 2),  # 0–10
        "recommendation":   classify(weighted),
    }

def classify(score: float) -> str:
    if score >= 7.5:  return "consolidate_immediately"
    if score >= 5.0:  return "consolidate_with_plan"
    if score >= 3.0:  return "scope_limit"
    return "maintain_intentionally"
Applying the Model: Worked Examples
The following worked examples show how the scoring model produces consistent recommendations across different types of overlaps.

candidates = [
    ConsolidationCandidate(
        capability="text_generation",
        tool_ids=["anthropic-api", "azure-openai", "aws-bedrock"],
        capability_overlap="high",       # all three do LLM text gen
        combined_spend_band=">10k",      # $14,200/month combined
        compliance_status="no_dpa_only", # Bedrock lacks DPA
        migration_effort="low",          # all use similar REST APIs
        adoption_level="high",           # 18 teams
        notes="Internal platform gateway can front all three; migrate via gateway config"
    ),
    ConsolidationCandidate(
        capability="code_generation",
        tool_ids=["azure-openai-via-api", "github-copilot"],
        capability_overlap="medium",     # both do code gen, but Copilot is IDE-integrated
        combined_spend_band="2-5k",
        compliance_status="compliant",
        migration_effort="high",         # Copilot IDE integration != API call
        adoption_level="medium",
        notes="Copilot for IDE use; Azure OpenAI for programmatic/CI use — different surfaces"
    ),
    ConsolidationCandidate(
        capability="embeddings",
        tool_ids=["azure-openai", "cohere"],
        capability_overlap="high",
        combined_spend_band="500-2k",
        compliance_status="no_dpa_only", # Cohere lacks DPA
        migration_effort="trivial",      # same float[] output; dimension change needed
        adoption_level="low",
        notes="Migrate Cohere users to Azure OpenAI text-embedding-3-small"
    ),
]

results = [score(c) for c in candidates]
for r in sorted(results, key=lambda x: -x["weighted_score"]):
    print(f"{r['candidate']:20s}  score={r['weighted_score']:.1f}  → {r['recommendation']}")

# Output:
# text_generation       score=7.9  → consolidate_immediately
# embeddings            score=7.6  → consolidate_immediately
# code_generation       score=4.1  → scope_limit
When Not to Consolidate

Not every overlap is a problem. Several patterns indicate that maintaining separate tools is the right decision, and the consolidation framework should make these cases visible rather than forcing consolidation by default.
Intentional multi-vendor resilience.

If the organisation has an explicit policy of maintaining two approved LLM providers to ensure continuity in case one provider has an outage or changes pricing, the overlap is intentional. Document this as a 'maintain intentionally' decision in the rationalization record, with the resilience rationale written out. Revisit annually.
Data residency or sovereignty constraints.

A tool that processes data only within a specific jurisdiction may coexist with a general-purpose tool without overlap, even if the capabilities look similar. The use case is defined by the data classification rules, not by the API features.
Model-specific capability that the primary tool lacks.

Some models have documented strengths in specific domains — a model that outperforms the standard tool for a specific task (e.g. long-context document analysis, a specific language, code in a niche framework) is differentiated, even if the general capability category is the same.
Contractual lock-in with migration cost exceeding savings.

If a tool has a multi-year enterprise agreement with significant early-termination penalties, and the annual savings from consolidation are less than the termination cost, the financial case for immediate consolidation does not exist. The correct action is to plan for consolidation at contract renewal, not before.
The Consolidation Brief

Each consolidation decision is documented in a one-page consolidation brief that the Platform Lead submits to the rationalization review. The brief prevents the review from becoming a live debate about facts that should have been established in advance.

# Consolidation Brief: Text Generation — LLM APIs

**Overlap group:** Anthropic API + Azure OpenAI + AWS Bedrock
**Capability:** Text generation (chat completion, instruction following)
**Prepared by:** GenAI Platform Lead | Review date: 2025-Q3

## Financial summary
| Vendor        | Monthly spend | Teams | Contract end |
|---------------|--------------|-------|--------------|
| Anthropic API | $6,200        | 11    | 2026-01-31   |
| Azure OpenAI  | $5,100        | 14    | 2025-12-31   |
| AWS Bedrock   | $2,900        | 6     | pay-per-use  |
| **Combined**  | **$14,200**   | **18** |             |

Post-consolidation target: $6,500–7,000/month via internal gateway
Projected annual saving: ~$87,000

## Capability differentiation assessment
- Anthropic (Claude) and Azure OpenAI (GPT-4o): functionally equivalent for all
  18 team use cases reviewed. No team identified a task that only one of these models
  could complete.
- AWS Bedrock: access to Claude models via Bedrock — same capability as Anthropic
  direct API, with no DPA on file. No unique capability justifies the separate subscription.

## Compliance status
- Anthropic API: DPA signed; security DD complete; approved for production.
- Azure OpenAI: DPA signed; security DD complete; approved for production.
- AWS Bedrock: DPA NOT signed. Data processing via Bedrock is a compliance risk
  pending DPA completion. No production use approved.

## Recommended action
**Consolidate to internal gateway** routing to Anthropic API (primary) + Azure OpenAI
(secondary, for enterprise customers requiring Azure data residency).
Deprecate direct Bedrock access within 60 days. Bedrock DPA to be completed regardless
for future potential use.

## Migration plan summary
1. Internal gateway updated to route to both approved providers (already live).
2. Teams using Bedrock: redirect to internal gateway endpoint (< 1 hour per team).
3. Teams using Anthropic or Azure OpenAI directly: migrate to gateway for centralised
   rate-limiting, cost attribution, and audit logging (1 sprint per team).
4. Direct API keys revoked after all teams confirmed migrated.

## Risk
- Gateway availability: if gateway has >99.5% SLA, direct API as fallback is acceptable
  during transition. After transition, gateway owns the resilience.
- Model preference: 3 teams expressed preference for specific model versions.
  Gateway model routing already supports model pinning at team level.

## Decision requested
Approve consolidation to internal gateway. Approve 60-day Bedrock off-board deadline.
Decision Record and Audit Trail

Every consolidation decision — whether to consolidate, scope-limit, or maintain intentionally — must be recorded. Without a written record, future team members will re-litigate settled decisions, and auditors will have no evidence of deliberate governance.
Field
Content
Decision date
ISO date of the rationalization review where the decision was made
Capability
The capability area the decision covers (from the canonical capability list)
Tools assessed
All tool IDs in the overlap group at assessment time
Decision
One of: consolidate_immediately | consolidate_with_plan | scope_limit | maintain_intentionally
Standard tool(s)
Which tool(s) are designated as the standard after the decision
Rationale
2–4 sentences explaining the basis for the decision, referencing the five dimensions
Migration deadline
If consolidation: the date by which all teams must have migrated
Next review date
When this decision is scheduled for re-evaluation (default: next annual review)
Decision owners
Platform Lead name; Legal sign-off (if compliance-driven); CISO sign-off (if security-driven)

A decision record is not optional. If the rationalization review approves a consolidation but no decision record is written within 5 business days, the decision is treated as not made and the review must re-examine the case. Decision records are the governance artefact; the meeting is where they are agreed, not where they are created.
Integration with Contract Management

Consolidation decisions are only durable if they are reflected in contract management. A decision to consolidate at renewal has no effect if procurement is not informed in time to act at the renewal date.
90-day renewal flag.

Every tool in the inventory with a consolidation decision of 'consolidate_with_plan' or 'scope_limit' must be flagged in the contract management system with a 90-day renewal review requirement. When the renewal flag fires, the Platform Lead confirms whether the consolidation has been completed (in which case, the contract is not renewed) or whether a timeline extension is needed.
Do-not-renew list.

Tools classified as 'consolidate_immediately' that are not yet off-boarded at the time of renewal review are escalated to the CISO and CFO. Procurement does not renew or extend without an explicit exception approved at that level.
New contract review.
Any new AI tool contract must include the inventory record ID in the contract metadata. Procurement uses this linkage to confirm that the tool passed the overlap check before the contract was signed.
Spend reconciliation.

After consolidation, Finance confirms the terminated vendor's invoices have stopped. A tool that is 'deprecated' in the decision record but still being invoiced indicates that the contract was not formally terminated — a procurement failure that must be escalated.
Running the Quarterly Rationalization Review

The rationalization review is the forum where consolidation decisions are made. Without a defined agenda and pre-work expectations, it degrades into a discussion of individual tool preferences rather than a governance meeting with decision authority.
Pre-work (Platform Lead, 1 week before)
Run the inventory refresh scan. Update all tool records with current spend, user counts, and compliance status.
Score all active overlap candidates using the scoring model. Sort by weighted score descending.
For each overlap scoring ≥ 5.0, prepare a consolidation brief (one page per overlap group).

Send briefs to Legal, Security, Finance, and relevant Engineering Team Leads 5 days before the review. Request that they come prepared with objections or additional data, not questions about basic facts.
Prepare a dashboard showing: number of active overlaps, combined spend across overlap groups, number of tools with compliance gaps, open migration deadlines from prior decisions.
Meeting agenda (90 minutes)
Time
Item
Owner
Expected output
Dashboard review
Platform Lead
Everyone aligned on the current state of the inventory
Confirm prior decision status
Platform Lead
Confirm migrations from prior decisions are on track or flag slippage
New consolidation decisions
Platform Lead (facilitates), Legal, Security as required
Decision recorded for each overlap scoring ≥ 5.0; deferred overlaps < 5.0 noted
Exception review
Engineering Team Leads who filed exceptions
Exceptions approved, denied, or time-limited
Contract management sync
Finance / Procurement
Renewals, terminations, and new registrations confirmed for next quarter
After the review
Decision records written within 5 business days by the Platform Lead.
Contract management system updated: renewal flags, do-not-renew entries, new inventory registrations.
Migration owners notified of their deadlines with a written brief, not just a verbal mention in the meeting.
Next review scheduled before the current review ends — date is a standing calendar invite, not rescheduled quarterly.
Common Decision Failures
Failure mode
What happens
Prevention
Deciding without data
Review runs without up-to-date spend, user counts, or compliance status. Decisions are based on impressions.
Platform Lead mandates inventory refresh and brief preparation as non-negotiable pre-work. Meeting is cancelled if briefs are not ready.
Team veto by preference
A team lead says 'we prefer this tool' and the overlap is not resolved. The meeting accepts anecdote as evidence.
Scoring model makes preference insufficient — teams must present scored evidence that the 'preferred' tool is differentiated, not just liked.
Deferred indefinitely
Consolidation decisions are marked 'review next quarter' repeatedly. No migration ever starts.
Decisions deferred more than twice are escalated to the CISO or CFO as a risk acceptance decision, not a platform decision.
Migration deadline set, no follow-through
A deadline is set at the review, but no one checks whether it was met.
Platform Lead owns a migration tracker. Missed deadlines are reported in the next review's dashboard with the team lead named.
Compliance gap treated as low priority
A tool with no DPA is discussed alongside compliant tools and given the same migration timeline.
Compliance gaps immediately trigger a shorter track: 60-day deadline vs. standard 1–2 quarter timeline.
New tools added without overlap check
Post-review, a team purchases a new tool that duplicates a just-consolidated capability.
Procurement system blocks new AI tool purchase orders that lack an inventory record with an overlap-check clearance flag.
Summary

Tool consolidation decisions require a consistent framework that evaluates five dimensions — capability overlap, combined spend, compliance status, migration cost, and adoption depth — rather than defaulting to the preferences of the loudest team in the review. A weighted scoring model turns these assessments into a numeric priority that makes the order of action explicit and defensible. The consolidation brief packages the findings into a one-page artefact that gives the rationalization review everything it needs to decide without re-deriving facts in the meeting. Every decision is recorded with a named decision owner, a migration deadline, and a next-review date. A structured quarterly review with defined pre-work, a timed agenda, and follow-through tracking prevents the common failure modes — decision deferral, preference-based vetoes, and compliance gaps tolerated indefinitely. Consolidation decisions that are not wired into contract management and procurement controls are advisory only — they must result in contract terminations or renewals-denied to have actual impact on the organisation's tool portfolio.
